header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: The Language of Culture -- Diagnosing and Describing Culture
lesson.menu_desc: Third conditional, modal perfects e o vocabulário do diagnóstico cultural -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 2 -- The Language of Culture
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Aula 2 -- The Language of Culture

listenings.text: Thank you for making time for this. I have thought a lot about what I would say in an exit interview, and I decided to be honest. I did not leave because of the salary. I left because nobody looked into what was actually happening on my team. We had core values on the wall, and none of them survived the first difficult meeting. If someone had asked me this question a year ago, I would have told you exactly the same thing, and the answer would have cost the company nothing. Instead you waited until I resigned. My onboarding experience was excellent, by the way. Two people welcomed me, the framework was clear, and I was proud to be here. Then the first project went wrong, and the person who spoke up in that meeting was blamed in front of everyone. After that, nobody spoke up again. That is the moment I stopped caring, and I was not the only one.

Danielle, it is Nathan. I have the pulse survey results in front of me, and I want you to see them before we meet. Two numbers stand out. Belonging is at seventy eight percent, which is honestly higher than I expected for an academic team. Psychological safety is at forty one percent. Forty one. So people feel they are part of something, and they still do not feel safe enough to disagree with a senior colleague in a room. Now, here is what I want from you. Do not arrive and tell us we have a toxic culture. We do not, and you would lose the room in thirty seconds. What we have is one specific problem that nobody names out loud, and I would like you to name it. Look into the numbers first, take a stance, and bring me a diagnosis. Not a plan. A diagnosis.

paras: Northline Academy was a network of bilingual schools with an excellent reputation and a serious problem. In three years, its turnover rate had climbed from nine percent to twenty six percent, and the exit interviews all told the same story. The company had written a beautiful set of core values, printed them, framed them, and hung them in every corridor. It had never once carried out a cultural diagnosis to find out whether anyone actually lived by them.

The warning signs were there long before the numbers moved. Managers stopped disagreeing with their directors in meetings. Two of the best academic coordinators resigned in the same quarter and gave polite, empty reasons. The onboarding experience was excellent for the first week and abandoned by the second. The people who stayed did their work, delivered the curriculum, and quietly stopped bringing new ideas. That is disengagement, and it is much more expensive than the attrition it produces.

When the board finally asked what had gone wrong, the answer was uncomfortable. If leadership had run a pulse survey in that first year, they would have seen psychological safety collapsing. If someone had looked into the exit interviews instead of filing them, they would have found the elephant in the room by the second one. The culture had not failed because it was toxic. It had failed because nobody was willing to name what everybody could already see.
reading.source: Case study adapted for Alumni -- Northline Academy (fictional)

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the case?
choices: Northline Academy lost people because its salaries were below the market.
The culture failed because nobody diagnosed it, and nobody named the problem everyone could see.
The company had no core values, so employees had nothing to believe in.

items: Northline Academy had written core values.
They wrote them, framed them and hung them in every corridor -- they simply never checked whether anyone lived by them.
The company ran a cultural diagnosis in the first year.
It never carried one out. That is precisely the gap the case is about.
Disengagement showed up before the turnover rate moved.
Managers stopped disagreeing and people stopped bringing ideas long before anyone resigned.
The case says the culture was toxic.
It says the opposite: the culture failed because nobody named the obvious problem, not because it was toxic.

items.situation: "Our turnover rate doubled last year. What went wrong?"
tips: If they had run a pulse survey, they would have seen it coming.
third conditional -- a past that did not happen

items.situation: "You have read our survey. Is our culture toxic?"
tips: It would appear that the issue is more specific than that.
To some extent, yes -- but the data points to psychological safety, not toxicity.

items.situation: "Two coordinators resigned last quarter. Could we have kept them?"
tips: We should have looked into the exit interviews.
They might not have left if someone had asked the question earlier.

items.situation: "Name the elephant in the room for us."
tips: People feel they belong, and they still do not feel safe enough to disagree.
take a stance -- name it, then support it with the number

items.situation: "What is the first thing you would change about our onboarding experience?"
tips: I would look into what happens in week two, not week one.
Before I change anything, I want to carry out a cultural diagnosis.

items.situation: "How do we foster a culture of speaking up?"
tips: You foster a culture of speaking up by protecting the first person who does it.
Psychological safety is built in the meeting where someone disagrees with you.
